brief_justification: Paper A's machinery is the Method of Regions: asymptotic expansion of gauge-theory loop integrals into scaling regions (hard/soft/collinear/Glauber), with 'hidden regions' identified by a scaling algorithm to recover factorisation-violating splitting amplitudes. Paper B's machinery is real-variable a priori estimation for the Stokes boundary-value problem: a nonlinear gradient quantity yielding a global second-order estimate and reverse Hoelder inequality to extend L^p solvability on Lipschitz domains. These are different mechanisms with no role-for-role correspondence (no shared governing equation, threshold, or estimation principle); the only overlap is the generic word 'limit/asymptotic'.
